result.markdown:
--- title: "Shiny Dashboards & Theming" author: "Joe Nese" institute: "University of Oregon
Spring 2026" format: revealjs: theme: default highlight-style: github incremental: false fig-width: 9 fig-height: 3.5 scrollable: true slide-number: true execute: echo: true warning: false embed-resources: true --- # Shiny Dashboards & Theming **Week 10** ## Agenda {.smaller} - Shiny Dashboards + `{shinydashboard}` & `{bslib}` - What is `{bslib}` - Theming + CSS with `bs_theme()` + Figures with `{thematic}` - Some shiny best practices **Learning objectives** * Be able to create basic `shiny` dashboards * Be able to theme your `shiny` apps and figures * Recognize use cases where your functional programming skills can help make more efficient and/or clear apps ## Housekeeping Please complete the Student Experience Survey! # Shiny Dashboards ## Two options {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} `{shinydashboard}` - classic approach (Bootstrap 3) - Compared to Shiny + You now have `dashboardPage()`, `dashboardSidebar()`, `dashboardBody()` + You also now have `fluidRow` and `box` arguments to arrange things in the main body ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} `{bslib}` - modern approach (Bootstrap 5) - Compared to Shiny + The same functions! + Built into `{shiny}` - Better theming ::: :::: ## `{shinydashboard}` {.smaller} ::: {.panel-tabset} ## `ui` {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .incremental} - `dashboardHeader()` — title bar - `dashboardSidebar()` — navigation menu + defining characteristic + Define a `sidebarMenu()` with `menuItem()`s + Each `menuItem()` needs a `tabName` that matches a `tabItem()` in the body + This should be how you control/refer to the `menuItem()` - `dashboardBody()` — main content area + `fluidRow()` and `box()` to lay out content + create `tabItems()` with specific `tabItem()` pieces ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|4|5|7|17|8|9,11|18,19,25|20-22,26"} library(shiny) library(shinydashboard) ui <- dashboardPage( dashboardHeader(title = "My Dashboard"), dashboardSidebar( sidebarMenu( menuItem("Plots", tabName = "plots", icon = icon("chart-bar")), menuItem("Table", tabName = "table", icon = icon("table")), sliderInput("bins", "Bins:", 1, 50, 30) ) ), dashboardBody( tabItems( tabItem(tabName = "plots", fluidRow( box(plotOutput("hist"), width = 8), box(plotOutput("density"), width = 4) ) ), tabItem(tabName = "table", fluidRow(box(dataTableOutput("tbl"), width = 12)) ) ) ) ) ``` ::: ::: :::: ## `server` {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - The `server` is unchanged from a regular `Shiny` app - You can also do things like put the slider in the `sidebarMenu()` + Bonus - it can then control things across tabs! + This is the key advantage of putting inputs in `dashboardSidebar()` ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} server <- function(input, output, session) { # Data shared across tabs via reactive() filtered <- reactive({ faithful$waiting }) # Tab 1: Plots output$hist <- renderPlot({ hist(filtered(), breaks = input$bins, col = "steelblue", border = "white", main = "Waiting Times") }) output$density <- renderPlot({ plot(density(filtered()), main = "Density") }) # Tab 2: Table output$tbl <- renderDataTable({ data.frame(waiting = filtered()) }) } ``` ::: ::: :::: ## `shinyApp()` The same ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ::: # `{bslib}` build around Bootstrap (`bs`) ## What is Bootstrap? {.smaller} ::: {.incremental} - [Bootstrap](https://getbootstrap.com/) is a free, open-source frontend toolkit + We famously love free and open source! - Provices pre-built CSS styles, components, and plugins for responsive websites - Uses a 12-column responsive layout system + sounds familiar, say more - Ready-to-use UI elements + buttons, navbars, modals, cards, dropdowns, forms, alerts, tooltips, badges, **etc** - Hundreds of helper classes for spacing, typography, colors, flexbox, display, and positioning - Designed to look good on small screens and scale up - Sounds great, do I have to learn it? + Nope! ::: ## `{bslib}` A modern UI toolkit for `Shiny` and Quarto, built on top of Bootstrap - Custom theming of Shiny apps and Quarto documents + Apps can even be themed interactively in real-time! - Customizable Shiny **dashboards**! ## What does this mean? {.smaller} You get: - 🖥️ Beautiful, responsive **Shiny dashboards** out of the box + `ui` components (cards, value boxes, sidebars) work in other contexts (e.g., Quarto) - 🎨 Easy **custom theming** - ⚡Modern use of Bootstrap and Bootswatch + `Shiny` defaults to Bootstrap 3, but Bootstrap 5 is available + [Bootswatch](https://bootswatch.com/) provides free, open-source themes for Bootstrap ## `{bslib}` vs `{shiny}` {.smaller} `{bslib}` is **NOT** a replacement for `Shiny`, it just enhances the `ui` (only) `Shiny` apps and `{bslib}` share the same foundations | Feature | `Shiny` | `{bslib}` | |---|---|---| | `ui` / `server` structure | ✅ | ✅ | | `shinyApp()` to run | ✅ | ✅ | | All standard inputs | ✅ | ✅ | | All standard outputs | ✅ | ✅ | | Reactive programming | ✅ | ✅ | | Deployment (e.g., Connet Cloud) | ✅ | ✅ | ## `{bslib}` vs `{shiny}` {.smaller} | Feature | `Shiny` | `{bslib}` | |---|---|---| | Bootstrap version | 3 (old) | 5 (modern) | | Page layout functions | `fluidPage()`, `navbarPage()` | `page_sidebar()`, `page_navbar()` | | `page` | `shiny::*Page()` | `bslib::page_*()` | | Theming | `shinythemes` (limited) | `bs_theme()` (fully custom) | | Cards | ❌ | `card()`, `card_header()` | | Value boxes | ❌ | `value_box()` | | Filling layouts | ❌ | `page_fillable()`, `fillable=TRUE` | | Real-time theming widget | ❌ | `bs_themer()` | ## `bslib::page_*()` functions | `Shiny` | `{bslib}` | |---|---| | `fluidPage()` | `page_fluid()` | `navbarPage()` | `page_navbar()` | `sidebarPanel()` | `page_sidebar()` ## `bslib::page_*()` functions `page_*()` for different layouts: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|4-5|7-9|11-13|15-16"} library(shiny) library(bslib) # Drop-in replacement for fluidPage() — gets modern Bootstrap 5 ui <- page_fluid(h2("Hello World")) # Dashboard with a sidebar ui <- page_sidebar(title = "My Dashboard", sidebar = "Controls", "Main content") # Multi-page navbar dashboard ui <- page_navbar(title = "My App", nav_panel("Page 1", "Content")) # For custom layout (not often used) ui <- page_fillable(...) ``` ::: # Let's build a dashboard! ## Dashboard `ui` basics {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="60%"} `page_sidebar()` takes three key arguments: - `title` + shown in the navbar - `sidebar` + anything placed in the left panel - `...` + main content (`cards`, plots, tables, **etc**) ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(shiny) library(bslib) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = sidebar(), ... ) ``` ::: ::: :::: ## Penguins Dashboard ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|6|8-16|17-20|23-31"} library(shiny) library(bslib) library(tidyverse) library(palmerpenguins) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = sidebar( title = "Histogram controls", varSelectInput( "var", "Select variable", dplyr::select_if(penguins, is.numeric) ), numericInput("bins", "Number of bins", 30) ), card( card_header("Histogram"), plotOutput("p") ) ) server <- function(input, output) { output$p <- renderPlot({ ggplot(penguins) + geom_histogram(aes(!!input$var), bins = input$bins) + theme_bw(base_size = 20) }) } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ## `sidebar()` `sidebar` content, styling, and behavior **`inputs` go here!** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sidebar( title = "Controls", # panel title bg = "white", # background color position = "left", # or "right" open = TRUE, # starts open? width = 250, # width in px # inputs go here too!! sliderInput(...), selectInput(...) ) ``` ::: ## `card()` A styled rectangular container, wraps output content in a bordered, rounded box __`*Outputs` go here!!__ ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} card( card_header("My Plot"), # optional header plotOutput("p"), # content card_footer("Source: ..."), # optional footer full_screen = TRUE, # expand button height = 400, # fixed height min_height = 200 # minimum height ) ``` ::: ## Let's Try Something {.smaller} We have similar outputs for multiple variables, each with a `plotOutput()` within a `card()` Let's make reusable outputs in a list [demo]{style="color:#009E73;"} ::: {.panel-tabset} ## Rusable `card`s ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cards <- list( card( full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Bill Length"), plotOutput("bill_length") ), card( full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Bill Depth"), plotOutput("bill_depth") ), card( full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Body Mass"), plotOutput("body_mass") ) ) ``` ::: ## `ui` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|8"} ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins dashboard", sidebar = varSelectInput( "color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species", "island", "sex")], selected = "species" ), !!!cards # <-- unpacks all 3 cards into the main area ) ``` ::: ## `server` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} server <- function(input, output) { gg_plot <- reactive({ ggplot(penguins) + geom_density(aes(fill = !!input$color_by), alpha = 0.2) + theme_bw(base_size = 16) + theme(axis.title = element_blank()) }) output$bill_length <- renderPlot(gg_plot() + aes(bill_length_mm)) output$bill_depth <- renderPlot(gg_plot() + aes(bill_depth_mm)) output$body_mass <- renderPlot(gg_plot() + aes(body_mass_g)) } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ::: ## Multiple Pages {.smaller} Change `page_sidebar()` to `page_navbar()` Add `nav_panel()` for each page ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|1|9-11"} ui <- page_navbar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = varSelectInput( # shared across all pages "color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species", "island", "sex")], selected = "species" ), nav_spacer(), # pushes nav items to the right nav_panel("Bill Length", cards[[1]]), nav_panel("Bill Depth", cards[[2]]), nav_panel("Body Mass", cards[[3]]), ) ``` ::: . . . The `sidebar` argument within `page_navbar()` puts the **same sidebar on every page** For a different sidebar on different pages, use `layout_sidebar()` within each `nav_panel()` ## Multiple Panels One Page Use `navset_card_*()` to create tabbed panels inside a single card: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|8"} ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = varSelectInput( "color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species", "island", "sex")], selected = "species" ), navset_card_pill( title = "Histograms by species", nav_panel("Bill Length", plotOutput("bill_length")), nav_panel("Bill Depth", plotOutput("bill_depth")), nav_panel("Body Mass", plotOutput("body_mass")) ) ) ``` ::: ## Multiple Panels - `navset_card_underline()` + underline tabs on a card - `navset_card_pill()` + pill-style tabs on a card - `navset_tab()` + classic Bootstrap tabs - `navset_pill()` + pill navigation ## `value_box()` Highlight key metrics with Bootstrap [icons](https://icons.getbootstrap.com/) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|6|7|8-13|14-19|20-25|27-28"} library(bsicons) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = color_by, layout_columns( fill = FALSE, # <-- prevents value boxes from stretching value_box( title = "Average bill length", value = scales::unit_format(unit = "mm")(mean(penguins$bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE)), showcase = bs_icon("align-bottom"), theme = "primary" ), value_box( title = "Average bill depth", value = scales::unit_format(unit = "mm")(mean(penguins$bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE)), showcase = bs_icon("align-center"), theme = "success" ), value_box( title = "Average body mass", value = scales::unit_format(unit = "g", big.mark = ",")(mean(penguins$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE)), showcase = bs_icon("handbag"), theme = "info" ) ), layout_columns(cards[[1]], cards[[2]]), cards[[3]] ) ``` ::: ## `{bslib}` Bonus! {.smaller} `{bslib}` works in **any** Shiny app, not just dashboards ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Upgrade any existing Shiny app to Bootstrap 5 ui <- page_fluid( theme = bs_theme(version = 5), titlePanel("My App"), sidebarLayout( sidebarPanel(...), mainPanel(...) ) ) ``` ::: . . . Also works in Quarto 🤞 ```yaml --- output: html_document: theme: bslib: true --- ``` ## `{bslib}` Bonus! `{bslib}` works anywhere Bootstrap is used - Static HTML - `{flexdashboard}` - `{pkgdown}` sites - `{bookdown}` books # `{bslib}` Functions References ## Page Layout Functions | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `page_sidebar()` | Single-page with left sidebar | | `page_navbar()` | Multi-page navbar dashboard | | `page_fluid()` | Drop-in for `fluidPage()` (Bootstrap 5) | | `page_fillable()` | Filling layout foundation | | `page_fixed()` | Drop-in for `fixedPage()` | ## Component Functions | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `card()` | Bordered content container | | `card_header()` | Card header with optional title | | `card_footer()` | Card footer | | `value_box()` | Single metric highlight | | `sidebar()` | Sidebar panel with options | | `layout_sidebar()` | Sidebar at the component level | | `accordion()` | Collapsible sections container | | `accordion_panel()` | Single collapsible section | ## Layout Functions | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `layout_columns()` | Multi-column, 12-column grid | | `layout_column_wrap()` | Responsive auto-wrapping columns | | `breakpoints()` | Responsive breakpoint values | ## Navigation Functions | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `nav_panel()` | A single tab/page | | `nav_spacer()` | Flexible space in navbar | | `nav_item()` | Custom UI item in navbar | | `nav_menu()` | Dropdown menu in navbar | | `navset_card_underline()` | Underline tabs on a card | | `navset_card_pill()` | Pill tabs on a card | | `navset_tab()` | Classic Bootstrap tabs | # Theming ## `bs_theme()` {.smaller} Pass it to the `theme` argument of any `page_*()` function ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|3-11"} ui <- page_sidebar( title = "My App", theme = bs_theme( bootswatch = "darkly", # Bootswatch preset theme bg = "#101010", # background color fg = "#FFF", # foreground/text color primary = "#E69F00", # primary accent color secondary = "#0072B2", # secondary accent color base_font = font_google("Inter"), code_font = font_google("JetBrains Mono") ), ... ) ``` ::: . . . This is different than plot themes, which we'll get to ## Color Arguments {.smaller} | Argument | Affects | |---|---| | `bg` | Cards, panels, modals, inputs | | `fg` | Foreground/text — affects ALL text | | `primary` | Links, active nav, focus rings, action buttons | | `secondary` | `actionButton()` default, secondary elements | | `success` | Success messages, `showNotification(type="message")` | | `info` | Info callouts | | `warning` | Warning messages | | `danger` | Error messages | ## Bootswatch Themes {.smaller} Default [Bootswatch](https://bootswatch.com) themes are great (let's take a look) `Shiny` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} page_sidebar( theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "minty"), ...) ``` ::: Quarto ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} --- output: html_document: theme: bootswatch: minty --- ``` ::: ## Bootswatch Themes {.smaller} | Theme | Description | |---|---| | `flatly` | Clean, modern, flat | | `darkly` | Dark mode | | `minty` | Soft mint green | | `cosmo` | Clean Bootstrap 5 default | | `cyborg` | Dark, futuristic | | `lumen` | Bright, airy | | `solar` | Solarized dark | | `zephyr` | Modern, fresh | | `morph` | Soft, Material-adjacent | ## Custom Fonts {.smaller} Use any [Google Font](https://fonts.google.com/) with `font_google()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bs_theme( base_font = font_google("Inter"), # body text heading_font = font_google("Playfair Display"), # headings code_font = font_google("JetBrains Mono") # code blocks ) ``` ::: . . . Font suggestions: - `base_font`: serif fonts - `heading_font`: sans-serif or display fonts - `code_font`: monospace fonts + Atkinson Hyperlegible ## Other font options: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # No download needed bs_theme(base_font = '"Times New Roman", Times, serif') # Custom font from a URL bs_theme(base_font = font_link("MyFont", href = "https://...")) # Custom font from local files bs_theme(base_font = font_face("MyFont", src = "fonts/myfont.woff2")) ``` ::: ## Advanced Theming {.smaller} Access hundreds of [{bslib} theming variables](https://rstudio.github.io/bslib/articles/bs5-variables) via `...` in `bs_theme()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bs_theme( bg = "#002B36", fg = "#EEE8D5", "progress-bar-bg" = "orange" # any Bootstrap Sass variable ) ``` ::: . . . Variables use Bootstrap Sass names (no `$`, no `!default`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bs_theme( "border-radius" = "0", # square corners everywhere "font-size-base" = "1.1rem", # slightly larger base font "enable-rounded" = FALSE, # disable all rounded corners "card-border-color" = "#ddd", # card border color "navbar-padding-y" = "1rem" # taller navbar ) ``` ::: ## Theme anything {.smaller} `bs_theme()` works in **ANY** Shiny app via `fluidPage()` or `page_fluid()` Old `{shinythemes}` way (Bootstrap 3 only) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- fluidPage( theme = shinythemes::shinytheme("darkly"), ... ) ``` ::: Modern `{bslib}` way (Bootstrap 5, fully customizable) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- page_fluid( theme = bs_theme( bootswatch = "darkly", primary = "#E69F00", base_font = font_google("Inter") ), ... ) ``` ::: . . . Or Quarto ```yaml --- output: html_document: theme: version: 5 bootswatch: minty primary: "#6CC3D4" base_font: google: "Inter" --- ``` ## Live theming! {.smaller} `bs_themer()` let's you interactively theme your app - Pick any Bootswatch theme from a dropdown - Adjust background, foreground, and accent colors - Change fonts - See changes **instantly** without restarting the app - Copy/Paste the resulting `bs_theme()` R code to your app ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|4"} ui <- page_sidebar(title = "My App", ...) server <- function(input, output) { bs_themer() # <-- add this line to the server function ... } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: [demo]{style="color:#009E73;"} . . . Or launch the demo app with active `bs_themer()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bs_theme_preview() ``` ::: # `{thematic}` ## The problem When you apply your custom CSS theme to a `Shiny` app, your plots don’t get the message For example, you applied a dark `bg` but now your plots look like white rectangles floating in space . . . **The solution** With `{thematic}`, your plots automatically pick up the CSS theme's colors and fonts And this works with `{ggplot2}` and `{base}` figures ## How it works `{thematic}` grabs CSS styles from the plot's HTML container and uses them to set new `R` plotting defaults 1. Reads background color + → sets plot panel/background color 2. Reads foreground color + → sets text, axis, grid line colors 3. Reads accent color (hyperlink color) + → sets geom colors, smooth lines 4. Optionally reads font-family + → sets `R` plot font ## How to apply it {.smaller} Call it before `shinyApp()` to enable theming for **all** `plotOutput()`s :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} Colors only (safest option)
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="6"} library(thematic) ui <- ... server <- function(input, output) {...} thematic_shiny() shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Colors & fonts
(requires `{showtext}` or `{ragg}`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="7"} library(thematic) library(showtext) ui <- ... server <- function(input, output) {...} thematic_shiny(font = "auto") shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ::: :::: ## This will set - `ggplot2::theme()` background/foreground defaults - A color-blind safe default for `scale_color_discrete()` - `geom` defaults (point color, line color) - Continuous scale defaults (using `bg`/`fg`/`accent` blend) - If `font = "auto"`, translate CSS fonts to `R` fonts ## Custom Fonts If you are using themes with custom fonts (like Google Fonts) You will likely need to install either `{showtext}` or `{ragg}` . . . For auto/custom font rendering in Shiny with `{ragg}` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} options(shiny.useragg = TRUE) ``` ::: ## Full Example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|29"} library(shiny) library(tidyverse) library(bslib) library(thematic) library(ragg) options(shiny.useragg = TRUE) ui <- fluidPage( theme = bs_theme( bg = "#002B36", fg = "#EEE8D5", primary = "#2AA198", base_font = font_google("Pacifico") ), tabsetPanel(type = "pills", tabPanel("ggplot", plotOutput("ggplot")), tabPanel("lattice", plotOutput("lattice")), tabPanel("base", plotOutput("base")) ) ) server <- function(input, output) { output$ggplot <- renderPlot({ ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl))) + geom_point(size = 3) }) output$lattice <- renderPlot({ lattice::show.settings() }) output$base <- renderPlot({ image(volcano) }) } thematic_shiny(font = "auto") shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ## Manually {.smaller} Instead of auto-detection, specify colors explicitly ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(thematic) library(tidyverse) thematic_on( bg = "#222222", fg = "white", accent = "#0CE3AC", font = "Oxanium" # Google Font ) ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, color = factor(cyl))) + geom_point() + ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(mtcars))) ``` ::: . . . | Function | Use case | |---|---| | `thematic_on()` | Enable for the rest of the `R` session | | `thematic_off()` | Disable | | `thematic_shiny()` | Enable for a `Shiny` app (per-app) | | `thematic_rmd()` | Enable for an RMarkdown doc | | `thematic_theme()` | One-off use for a single plot | ## Overriding `{thematic}` defaults {.smaller} `{thematic}` sets **global defaults** that can always be overridden plot-by-plot ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} thematic_on(bg = "#222222", fg = "white", accent = "#0CE3AC") # Global default from thematic ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) + geom_point() # Override just for this one plot ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) + geom_point() + theme(text = element_text(color = "purple")) # override ``` ::: . . . `{ggplot2}` themes (e.g., `theme_bw()`, `theme_minimal()`) should always be set globally with `theme_set()` when using `{thematic}` This ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} theme_set(theme_minimal()) ``` ::: **NOT** this ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(...) + theme_minimal() ``` ::: ## Match your RStudio theme `{thematic}` can also match your RStudio IDE theme ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(thematic) thematic_on() ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) + geom_point() # Turn it off when done thematic_off() ``` ::: [Try it!]{style="color:#009E73;"} ## Example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(shiny) library(bslib) library(tidyverse) library(thematic) library(penguins) # Set ggplot2 global theme theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 15)) # Define UI with theme ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins Dashboard", sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", varSelectInput("color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species","island","sex")], selected = "species") ), theme = bs_theme( bootswatch = "darkly", base_font = font_google("Inter"), navbar_bg = "#25443B" # custom navbar background ), layout_columns( value_box("Avg Bill Length", "45.0 mm", showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("rulers")), value_box("Avg Bill Depth", "17.1 mm", showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("arrows-vertical")), value_box("Avg Body Mass", "4201.7 g", showcase = bsicons::bs_icon("bar-chart")) ), layout_columns( card(full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Bill Length"), plotOutput("bill_length")), card(full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Bill Depth"), plotOutput("bill_depth")) ), card(full_screen = TRUE, card_header("Body Mass"), plotOutput("body_mass")) ) server <- function(input, output) { gg <- reactive({ ggplot(penguins) + geom_density(aes(fill = !!input$color_by), alpha = 0.2) }) output$bill_length <- renderPlot(gg() + aes(bill_length_mm)) output$bill_depth <- renderPlot(gg() + aes(bill_depth_mm)) output$body_mass <- renderPlot(gg() + aes(body_mass_g)) } # Enable thematic to carry the theme into R plots thematic::thematic_shiny(font = "auto") shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ## Production Tip {.smaller} Hard-code the Bootstrap version in case `{bslib}` updates Bootstrap in the future Check which version is current ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bslib::version_default() # e.g., "5" ``` ::: Set it in your theme ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- page_sidebar( theme = bs_theme(version = 5, ...), ... ) ``` ::: . . . In Quarto ```yaml --- output: html_document: theme: version: 5 --- ``` ## Quick Reference {.smaller} When to use what | Goal | Use | |---|---| | Build a dashboard | `page_sidebar()` or `page_navbar()` | | Add Bootswatch theme | `bs_theme(bootswatch = "...")` | | Custom dark/light mode | `bs_theme(bg=, fg=)` | | Google Fonts | `font_google("Font Name")` | | Fine-grained Sass vars | `bs_theme("variable-name" = value)` | | Interactive theming | `bs_themer()` in server | | Match `R` plots to CSS | `thematic_shiny()` | | Match plots in RStudio | `thematic_on()` | | One-off plot theming | `thematic_theme()` | ## Resources {.smaller} - `{bslib}` documentation: [rstudio.github.io/bslib](https://rstudio.github.io/bslib/) - [Dashboards](https://rstudio.github.io/bslib/articles/dashboards/) - [Theming](https://rstudio.github.io/bslib/articles/theming/) - [Theming variables](https://rstudio.github.io/bslib/articles/bs5-variables/) - `{thematic}` documentation: [rstudio.github.io/thematic](https://rstudio.github.io/thematic/) - [Auto-theming](https://rstudio.github.io/thematic/articles/auto.html) - Bootswatch themes: [bootswatch.com](https://bootswatch.com) - Google Fonts: [fonts.google.com](https://fonts.google.com) - Try the themer live: [bslib.shinyapps.io/themer-demo](https://bslib.shinyapps.io/themer-demo) # Some shiny tips > Any fool can write code that a computer can understand. Good programmers write code that humans can understand. \- Martin Fowler ## Things to consider * Are variable/function names clear and concise? * Is code commented, where needed? * Is there a way to break up complex functions? * Are there many instances of copy/pasting? * Can I manage different components of my app independently? Or are they too tangled together? ::: aside [Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/best-practices.html) ::: ## Increase readability Shiny apps can become unreadable **VERY** quickly Consider creating objects for each piece, possibly even in separate `R` files ## Example `color_by` In our Penguins dashboard, we used `varSelectInput()` in our sidebar ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|3-7"} ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins dashboard", sidebar = varSelectInput( "color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species", "island", "sex")], selected = "species" ), !!!cards # <-- unpacks all 3 cards into the main area ) ``` ::: ## Example `color_by` Make it an object ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|9"} color_by <- varSelectInput( "color_by", "Color by", penguins[c("species", "island", "sex")], selected = "species" ) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Penguins dashboard", sidebar = color_by, !!!cards # <-- unpacks all 3 cards into the main area ) ``` ::: ## Functions - You can `source()` to access functions **OR** - Create a new folder, `R/` - Place `.R` files in that folder - shiny will source them automatically!! [demo ISLA app]{style="color:#009E73;"} ## `ui` example Imagine we have a bunch of different sliders we want to create We could do something like this: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- fluidRow( sliderInput("alpha", "alpha", min = 0, max = 1, value = 0.5, step = 0.1), sliderInput("beta", "beta", min = 0, max = 1, value = 0.5, step = 0.1), sliderInput("gamma", "gamma", min = 0, max = 1, value = 0.5, step = 0.1), sliderInput("delta", "delta", min = 0, max = 1, value = 0.5, step = 0.1) ) ``` ::: . . . Ideas on how you could write a function to reduce the amount of code here? ## Slider function ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} my_slider <- function(id) { sliderInput(id, id, min = 0, max = 1, value = 0.5, step = 0.1) } ui <- fluidRow( my_slider("alpha"), my_slider("beta"), my_slider("gamma"), my_slider("delta") ) ``` ::: . . . Anyway to make this even less repetitive? ## Loop through the ids ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ids <- c("alpha", "beta", "gamma", "delta") sliders <- map(ids, my_slider) ui <- fluidRow(sliders) ``` ::: ## Other use cases - Maybe you want to use a shiny function, but assign it with some standard formatting + e.g., icons - Maybe there are functions where you only want to change 1-3 things? + Create a function that allows you to modify those, but keep other things at the values you want ## Server functions * I often find it helpful to create functions for output, even if I'm not repeating them a lot + Can help keep server code clean and concise * Inspect your code and consider refactoring in a similar way you would standard functions * Consider keeping these in a separate `.R` file from your `ui` functions # An app example ## Please follow along Let's build an app to show the results of different regression models And we'll use `bslib::page_sidebar()` for a polished dashboard layout ## First, an extension! We want to be able to select which variables are predictors in the model Ideally, we should be able to select 1, 2, ... $n$ predictors . . . `shinyWidgets::multiInput()` to the rescue! see the [widget gallery](https://shiny.posit.co/r/gallery/widgets/widget-gallery/) ## Super basic example {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|12-17"} library(shiny) library(shinyWidgets) library(bslib) library(palmerpenguins) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Example", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"), # pick any Bootswatch theme sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", multiInput( inputId = "xvars", label = "Select predictor variables:", choices = names(penguins)[-3], # column 3 will be outcome selected = "island" ) ) # main area content will go here ) ``` ::: ## Leave the server blank ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} server <- function(input, output) { } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: Try it! [demo]{style="color:#009E73;"} ## Write the `server` * We now want it to fit a model with `bill_length_mm` as the outcome, and whatever variables are selected as predictors * We can do this by creating a `reactive` model that only is estimated when something changes ## Model {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model <- reactive({ form <- paste( "bill_length_mm ~ ", paste(input$xvars, collapse = " + ") ) lm(as.formula(form), penguins) }) ``` ::: - The above creates a string `form`, which is then converted to a formula with `as.formula()` - It will only be estimated when `input$xvars` is changed - We can refer to the model object elsewhere in our server with `model()` + note the ending `()`! ## Build a table * Use the [gt](https://gt.rstudio.com/) and [gtsummary](https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/gtsummary/) packages to pull the model coefficients * Render the result in `gt` table * Remember to load additional packages ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|4-5"} library(shiny) library(shinyWidgets) library(palmerpenguins) library(gtsummary) library(gt) ``` ::: ## Table: `server` + `ui` {.smaller} In the `server` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} output$tbl <- render_gt({ gt::as_gt(gtsummary::tbl_regression(model(), intercept = TRUE)) }) ``` ::: . . . In the main area of the `ui` wrapped in `card()` so it sits neatly in the dashboard ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|13-16"} ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Example", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"), sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", multiInput( inputId = "xvars", label = "Select predictor variables:", choices = names(penguins)[-3], selected = "island" ) ), card( card_header("Model coefficients"), gt::gt_output("tbl") ) ) ``` ::: ## Try it! - Be sure to include + the `model` in the `server` + the `output$tbl` in the `server` + the `card()` in the `ui` [demo]{style="color:#009E73;"} ## Full ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|24-27|33-40|42-44"} library(shiny) library(shinyWidgets) library(bslib) library(palmerpenguins) library(gtsummary) library(gt) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Example", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"), ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Example", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"), sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", multiInput( inputId = "xvars", label = "Select predictor variables:", choices = names(penguins)[-3], selected = "island" ) ), card( card_header("Model coefficients"), gt::gt_output("tbl") ) ) ) server <- function(input, output) { model <- reactive({ form <- paste( "bill_length_mm ~ ", paste(input$xvars, collapse = " + ") ) lm(as.formula(form), penguins) }) output$tbl <- render_gt({ gt::as_gt(gtsummary::tbl_regression(model(), intercept = TRUE)) }) } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ## Make it fancy {.smaller} Let's add an equation with [equatiomatic](https://datalorax.github.io/equatiomatic/). You try first: * Use `extract_eq()` to extract the equation, and the `renderEq()` and `eqOutput()` functions for shiny + in the `server` * Make sure to include `withMathJax()` in your `ui` somewhere + I put it after the title * Check out the help page for `extract_eq()` for additional arguments, including `use_coefs` + play around with these options ``{=html} [demo]{style="color:#009E73;"} ## Additions {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|1|4-6|14|22-25"} library(equatiomatic) # In the server: output$eq <- renderEq( extract_eq(model(), use_coefs = TRUE) ) # In the ui main area — add a second card alongside the table: ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Example", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly"), sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", withMathJax(), # allows formula in shiny multiInput( inputId = "xvars", label = "Select predictor variables:", choices = names(penguins)[-3], selected = "island" ) ), card( # add a card to display the equation card_header("Model equation"), eqOutput("eq") ), card( card_header("Model coefficients"), gt_output("tbl") ) ) ``` ::: ## Full app ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(shiny) library(bslib) library(shinyWidgets) library(palmerpenguins) library(gtsummary) library(gt) library(equatiomatic) ui <- page_sidebar( title = "Regression Explorer", theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "flatly", base_font = font_google("Inter")), sidebar = sidebar( title = "Controls", withMathJax(), multiInput( inputId = "xvars", label = "Select predictor variables:", choices = names(penguins)[-3], selected = "island" ) ), card(card_header("Model equation"), eqOutput("eq")), card(card_header("Model coefficients"), gt_output("tbl")) ) server <- function(input, output) { model <- reactive({ form <- paste("bill_length_mm ~", paste(input$xvars, collapse = " + ")) lm(as.formula(form), penguins) }) output$eq <- renderEq(extract_eq(model(), use_coefs = TRUE)) output$tbl <- render_gt(as_gt(tbl_regression(model(), intercept = TRUE))) } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: ## `layout_columns()` Want the equation and table side-by-side? `col_widths` uses Bootstrap’s 12-column grid, so values must sum to 12 ::: {.cell code-line_numbers='|1,2,11'} ```{.r .cell-code} layout_columns( col_widths = c(5, 7), # out of 12 card( card_header("Model equation"), eqOutput("eq") ), card( card_header("Model coefficients"), gt_output("tbl") ) ) ``` ::: ## Going further {.smaller} - Partial regression plots? + see [visreg](https://pbreheny.github.io/visreg/) + just add another `card()` with `plotOutput()` - Change the outcome too? + Add a `selectInput()` to the sidebar (gets a bit tricky but doable) - Multi-page layout? + Switch `page_sidebar()` t0 `page_navbar()` + Use `nav_panel()` for "*Model*", "*Diagnostics*", "*About*" - Value boxes? + Show $R^2$, *RMSE*, $n$ at the top of the dashboard with `value_box()` - Theming? + Add `bs_themer()` before `shinyApp()` and explore themes live ## Conclusions * Shiny is fun! * Bigger apps get pretty complicated pretty quickly * Consider [modules](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html) * Do try to be efficient with your code whenever possible * Think carefully about reactivity - it's a fundamentally different approach ## Any time left? Challenge * Create a shiny app or shiny dashboard with the `palmerpenguins::penguins` dataset * Allow the *x* and *y* axes to be selected by the user + These should be any numeric variables * Allow the points to be colored by any categorical variable + For an added challenge, try to add in a "no color" option, which should be the default # Next time Final Exam due 6/8 @ 1pm # shiny modules {visibility="hidden"} (briefly) ## What is a module? {visibility="hidden"} * Allows you to modularize parts of your shiny app * This can help "de-tangle" these pices from the rest of the app * Primarily useful when the thing you want to modularize spans *both* the UI and the server ## Example {visibility="hidden"} No modules ![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/051011ce1a1bc8989ae41b735b05b607b2ebde65/2e519/diagrams/scaling-modules/before.png) ::: aside [Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html) ::: ## Example {visibility="hidden"} Same app, but with modules ![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/3d9a229fec717ef7bfda8e17a9c9d4fdc66b8b08/953f7/diagrams/scaling-modules/after.png) ::: aside [Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html) ::: ## Create modules {visibility="hidden"} * It's fairly complicated. See the [chapter](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html) from Mastering Shiny on the topic * Probably not worth it until you're creating complicated apps, but I wanted to make you aware of them . . . Let's look at a very basic use case from [Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html#module-basics) ## The original app {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- fluidPage( selectInput("var", "Variable", names(mtcars)), numericInput("bins", "bins", 10, min = 1), plotOutput("hist") ) server <- function(input, output, session) { data <- reactive(mtcars[[input$var]]) output$hist <- renderPlot({ hist( x = data(), breaks = input$bins, main = input$var ) }) } shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server) ``` ::: ## Histogram UI module {visibility="hidden"} * A function for the UI components of the histogram * Wrap all internals into a single function with an `id` argument * Wrap all inputs in a `tagList()`, with inputs separated by commmas * Wrap each ID in `NS()` + Allows the multiple id's to be referenced by a single id ## {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} uiHist <- function(id) { tagList( selectInput(NS(id, "var"), "Variable", names(mtcars)), numericInput(NS(id, "bins"), "bins", 10, min = 1), plotOutput(NS(id, "hist")) ) } ``` ::: ## Histogram server module {visibility="hidden"} * Wrap the internals in a function with an `id` argument * Inside this function, wrap the internals again with the `moduleServer()` function + First argument is `id` + Second argument is `function(input, output, session) { }` ## {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} serverHist <- function(id) { moduleServer(id, function(input, output, session) { data <- reactive(mtcars[[input$var]]) output$hist <- renderPlot({ hist( x = data(), breaks = input$bins, main = input$var ) }) }) } ``` ::: ## New app {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ui <- fluidPage( uiHist("histo") ) server <- function(input, output, session) { serverHist("histo") } ``` ::: . . . The modules can then live in their own files, and your UI and server functions become much more clean

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
